column1: #FORMAT=WebAnno TSV 3.3
#T_SP=custom.Span|label
#Text=From that time till Sunday, 4th March, our route lay along the coast, and we were never long together out of sight of the sea.
#Text=The coast is plain; and after having left the neighbourhood of Alexandria, where it is rocky, the soil is generally smooth and sandy.

#Text=Many spots of verdure, particularly at this season, relieve the eye from the effect of general barrenness: and though the vegetation be very inconsiderable, the greater part of it consisting only of different kinds of the grasswort, or kali, it offers a seasonable relief to the suffering camel.
#Text=For our horses we were obliged to carry a constant supply of barley and cut straw.
#Text=There are several kinds of preserved meat prepared among the orientals for long journies.
#Text=They obviate the inconveniency of salt provision by using clarified butter.
#Text=The kind most used is called mishli, and will keep good for many years.
#Text=It is brought from Western Barbary to Kahira.
#Text=In the places where we generally rested are found the jerboa, the tortoise, the lizard, and some serpents, but not in great number.
#Text=There is also an immense quantity of snails attached to the thorny plants on which the camels feed.
#Text=These the Arabs frequently eat.
#Text=Very few birds were visible in this quarter, except of the marine kind.
#Text=One of our party killed a small hawk, which was the only one I saw.

#Text=Near the few springs of water are found wild rabbits, which in Arabic they distinguish by the same name as the hare, (ارنب) and the track of the antelope and the ostrich are frequently discoverable.
#Text=We passed no day without being incommoded with frequent showers; and generally a cold wind from north-west and north-west by north.
#Text=Several small parties of Bedouins, who were feeding a few goats, sheep, and asses, were encamped in the road, and in the vicinity of the lake Mareotis, now dry.
#Text=Such of them as were the friends of our conductor received us with every mark of hospitality and kindness; and regaled us with milk, dates, and bread newly baked.
#Text=One party, indeed, became contentious for a present, or tribute on passing; but being in no condition to enforce their demand, it was after a time relinquished.
#Text=The Oasis which contains the town Siwa, is about six miles long, and four and a half or five wide.
#Text=A large proportion of this space is filled with date trees; but there are also pomegranates, figs, and olives, apricots and plantains; and the gardens are remarkable flourishing.
#Text=They cultivate a considerable quantity of rice, which, however, is of a reddish hue, and different from that of the Delta.
#Text=The remainder of the cultivable land furnishes wheat enough for the consumption of the inhabitants.

#Text=Water, both salt and fresh, abounds; but the springs which furnish the latter are most of them tepid; and such is the nature of the water, air, and other circumstances, that strangers are often affected with agues and malignant fevers.
#Text=One of those springs, which rises near the building described, is observed by the natives to be sometimes cold and sometimes warm.

#Text=The list of their household furniture is very short; some earthen ware made by themselves, and a few mats, form the chief part of it, none but the richer order being possessed of copper utensils.
#Text=They occasionally purchase a few slaves from the Murzouk caravan.

#Text=The remainder of their wants is supplied from Kahira or Alexandria, whither their dates are transported, both in a dry state, and beaten into a mass, which when good in some degree resembles a sweet meat.
#Text=They eat no large quantity of animal food; and bread of the kind known to us is uncommon.
#Text=Flat cakes, without leaven, kneaded, and then half baked, form part of their nourishment.
#Text=The remainder consists of thin sheets of paste, fried in the oil of the palm tree, rice, milk, dates, &c.
#Text=They drink in great quantities the liquor extracted from the date tree, which they term date-tree water, though it have often, in the state they drink it, the power of inebriating.
#Text=Their domestic animals are, the hairy sheep and goat of Egypt, the ass, and a very small number of oxen and camels.
#Text=The women are veiled, as in Egypt.
#Text=After the rains the ground in the neighbourhood of Siwa is covered with salt for many weeks.

#Text=Rîl[40] is the key of the South and East roads, as Cubcabîa of the West, and Sweini of the North; and therefore a Melek with a body of troops commonly resides there, as a guard to the frontier, and to keep the Arabs, who abound in that neighbourhood, in subjection.

#Text=It is a place eminently fitted for the Imperial residence, being abundantly supplied with fresh water from a large pool, which is never completely dry, with bread from Saïd[41], with meat, milk, and butter from the Arabs, who breed cattle, and with vegetables from a soil well adapted to horticulture; nor are they without a kind of tenacious clay, which, with little preparation, becomes a durable material for building.
#Text=In Shoba, another town of some note, was an house of Sultan Teraub.
#Text=The place is said to be well supplied with water, and there are some chalk pits near it, from which that material was drawn at the time I was in the country.
#Text=The Arabs and Jelabs find the camel too indispensable to their long and fatiguing voyages, not to employ much care in nourishing him.
#Text=This ship of the land, (مركب البر,) as he is called, is exclusively the bearer of their fortunes, and the companion of their toils.

#Text=Much care is observed in rearing him, and not unfrequently the merchant pays nearly as much for the camels to carry his merchandize, as he did for the commodities themselves; what then must be the profit that covers his expenses, his fatigue, indemnifies him for accidental losses, and yet leaves him a gainer?
#Text=But if this patient and enduring animal be thus rendered subservient to their wants, or their avarice, he is not at least tortured for their caprice.
#Text=Horses are very little used by the Jelabs.
#Text=They generally furnish themselves with Egyptian asses, which alleviate the fatigue of the way, and are afterwards sold in Soudân at an advanced price.

#Text=The strength and spirits of this animal are recruited with a small quantity of straw and water; the horse has not the same recommendation; and these people, though not averse from parade in cities, find the labour and hazard of these voyages too enormous, not to augment their profits by all possible economy.

#Text=The provisions they use are scanty and indifferent, and by no means testify any foresight for the necessities of the sick, or for the procrastination of the voyage by those innumerable accidents that may befal them.
#Text=I did not observe that any of them were furnished with dried meat, as is common with the Fezzanners.
#Text=But few used coffee and tobacco, and the rest contented themselves with a leathern bag of flour, another of bread baked hard, a leathern vessel of honey or treacle, and another of butter.
#Text=The quantity of each was regulated by the number of persons, and seldom exceeded what is absolutely necessary.
#Text=In travelling from Dar-Fûr to Egypt another article is much in use, especially for the slaves, which Egypt itself does not afford, or produces in no quantity.
#Text=The grain chiefly in use among the Fûrians is the small kassob, called among them dokn (millet).
#Text=Of this, after it has been coarsely ground, they take a quantity, and having caused it to undergo a slight fermentation, make a kind of paste.
#Text=This will keep a long time, and when about to be used, water is added to it; if properly made, it becomes a tolerably palatable food.
#Text=But the natives are not very delicate.
#Text=From its acidity they esteem it a preventive of thirst.
#Text=The fermentation gives it also a slight power of inebriating, and it has a narcotic tendency.
#Text=The substance so prepared is called ginseia.
#Text=The want of materials for fire on the road prevents the use of rice, and other articles that would require cookery.
#Text=Experienced travellers, among every ten camels laden with merchandize, charge one with beans, and straw chopped small, which, sparingly given, serves them during the greater part of the voyage.
#Text=Those with whom I travelled were not so provided, these articles being then very dear in Egypt; and in consequence numbers of camels perished.
#Text=In coming from Dar-Fûr, they use for the same purpose the dokn, and coarse hay of the country, but not altogether with the same salutary effect.
#Text=The water, in leaving Egypt, is commonly conveyed in goat skins artificially prepared; but no skill can entirely prevent evaporation.
#Text=On their march from Soudân to Egypt, the Jelabs oftener use ox-hides, formed into capacious sacks and properly seasoned with tar or oil.
#Text=A pair of these is a camel’s load.
#Text=They keep the water in a better state for drinking than the smaller; and these sacks are sold to great advantage throughout Egypt, a pair of the best kind being sometimes worth thirty piasters.
#Text=They are the common instruments for conveying water from the river to different parts of each town.

#Text=The camels are not allowed to partake of this store, which, after all the care that can be taken of it, is often very nauseous, from the tar, the mud which accompanies the water in drawing, heat, &c.
#Text=Six of the smaller skins, or two of the larger, are generally
#Text=esteemed sufficient for four persons for as many days.
#Text=The camels in this country are particularly  subject to the mange, (Gerab,) which attacks them chiefly in winter,  and in some pastures much more than in others.
#Text=This malady is very  contagious.
#Text=It is cured by the application of a kind of tar, procured  by distillation per deliquium, from the seeds of the water-melon.
#Text=When  the male camel is found unruly, they sometimes deprive him of one  or both testicles.
#Text=It is a cruel operation, as immediately after  having incided with an ordinary knife, they sear the wound with an  hot iron till the hemorrhage be stopped.
#Text=It may be doubted whether  this practice be permitted by the law of the Prophet; but, however  bigoted their minds, where advantage is hoped for, their faith is  ductile.
#Text=The flesh of the camel, particularly of the female, (naka,)  which is fattened for the purpose, is here much used for food.
#Text=It is  insipid, but easily digested, and no way unpleasant.
#Text=The milk also  is much in esteem.
#Text=A great number of insects and reptiles, which I had taken care to conserve, accident has deprived me of, and I cannot now furnish a catalogue.
#Text=The scorpion is small, of a brown hue, and his venom not extremely violent.
#Text=The natives cure the sting by immediately applying to the part a bruised onion, which is renewed till the pain subsides.
#Text=The locust of Arabia, Gryllus, is very common, and is frequently roasted and eaten, particularly by the slaves.
#Text=The Scarabeus Ceratoniæ; the Culex Egypti, namûs in Ar.
#Text=(mosquito), is particularly vexatious in the rainy season.
#Text=Nebbek Ar.
#Text=Paliurus Athenæi.
#Text=Of this there are two species in Dar-Fûr.
#Text=They term the largest Nebbek-el-arab.
#Text=There is a difference in their fruit, as well as in their external appearance.
#Text=The one is a bush, with leaves of dark green, not very different from those of the ivy, but much thinner.
#Text=It appeared to be the same I had seen in the gardens of Alexandria.
#Text=The other a tree, growing to considerable size, but having both the leaves and fruit smaller, and the fruit of darker colour, and somewhat different flavour.
#Text=Both of them equally thorny.
#Text=The natives eat the fruit fresh or dry; for it dries on the tree, and so remains great part of the winter months.
#Text=In that state it is formed into a paste of not unpleasant flavour, and is a portable provision on journies.
#Text=Heglîg or Hejlij, Ar.
#Text=This tree is about the same size as the one last mentioned, and is said to be a native of Arabia, though I have seen it only in Fûr.
#Text=—The leaf is small, and the fruit it bears is of an oblong form, about the size of a date.
#Text=Colour brown, tinctured with orange; dry, and of a viscous quality.
#Text=The nucleus is large in proportion to the fruit, which adheres to it with great tenacity.
#Text=This is also formed into a paste, but of no agreeable flavour.
#Text=It is however eaten by the Arabs, and by some esteemed efficacious as a remedy for certain diseases.
#Text=It seems a slight diuretic.
#Text=The wood is hard, and of a yellowish colour; it grows in great abundance, and is very thorny.
#Text=This, together with the Nebbek, chiefly furnish thorns for the fences.
#Text=Shaw, Ar. a shrub about the size of the Arbutus, having, like it, a leaf of strong texture, of oval form, pale green, wider at the lower, and narrower at the upper extremity than the arbutus.
#Text=—The leaf has the pungency and very much the taste of mustard.
#Text=This shrub I saw chiefly in Wadi Shaw, a place we passed in going and returning, between Sweini and Bîr-el-malha.
#Text=The natives cut off the smaller branches, which they use to rub their teeth, alleging that the acrid juice of this plant has the property of whitening them.
#Text=The harvest is conducted in a very simple manner.

#Text=The women and slaves of the proprietor are employed to break off the ears with their hands, leaving the straw standing, which is afterwards applied to buildings and various other useful purposes.
#Text=They then accumulate them in baskets, and carry them away on their heads.

#Text=When threshed, which is awkwardly and incompletely performed, they expose the grain to the sun till it become quite dry; after this an hole in the earth is prepared, the bottom and sides of which are covered with chaff to exclude the vermin.
#Text=This cavity or magazine is filled with grain, which is then covered with chaff, and afterwards with earth.
#Text=In this way the maize is preserved tolerably well.

#Text=In using it for food, they grind it, and boil it in the form of polenta, which is eaten either with fresh or sour milk, or still more frequently with a sauce made of dried meat pounded in a mortar, and boiled with onions, &c.
#Text=The Furians use little butter; with the Egyptians and Arabs it is an article in great request.

#Text=There is also another sauce which the poorer people use and highly relish, it is composed of an herb called Cowel or Cawel, of a taste in part acescent and in part bitter, and generally disagreeable to strangers.
#Text=As a substitute for bread, cakes of the same material are also baked on a smooth substance prepared for the purpose, which are extremely thin, and if dexterously prepared not unpalatable.

#Text=These are called kissery (fragments or sections); they are also eaten with the sauce above mentioned, or with milk, or simply water; and in whatever form the grain be used, the rich cause it to be fermented before it be reduced to flour, which gives it a very agreeable taste.
#Text=They also make no hesitation in eating the dokn raw, but moistened with water, without either grinding or the operation of fire.

#Text=The natives, who are unacquainted with the use of mercury, and indeed of minerals in general as employed internally, are yet provided, as they say, with efficacious remedies for the venereal disease.
#Text=They use flax oil, fresh, as it is expressed, from the seed.

#Text=A Greek, who was in the service of Murad Bey as a mariner, (galeongi,) and who was known to me in Kahira, had been infected, and on applying to a Frank physician, was told that it would be necessary immediately to use mercurials.
#Text=The man was not inclined to confinement or to regimen, and went to a Copt at Jizé, who professed to relieve the sick.
#Text=This man ordered him to take two coffee-cups of flax oil every morning fasting, and directed no regimen, but that of keeping himself warm.

#Text=The Greek observed none, for he continued freely the use of aqua vitæ, and even sacrificed to Venus, (for persons who have been once infected and fully cured, are, it is said, in no fear of reinfection,) and was often in the heat of the sun.
#Text=He had continued this method for two months, when a general eruption took place over his body, but chiefly about the head and glands of the throat.
#Text=In this condition I saw him.
#Text=His Esculapius ordered him to cover the pustules of his face with a kind of red earth, found in some parts of Egypt.
#Text=They gradually became dry, and came off without leaving any mark.

#Text=At the end of the third month from the time he had applied to the Copt, and one month after the appearance of the eruption, the man was in perfect health, and the skin had completely recovered its tone and polish.
#Text=In the cure of the simple gonorrhea, a decoction of mallows is commonly used, and they seem to place their chief confidence in diuretics.
#Text=I never heard of an injection, but from those who were acquainted with European practice.
#Text=Certain herbs and roots macerated, are applied locally in case of inflammation and tension (chordee).
#Text=Shankers, &c. externally, are repeatedly washed with soap and water, and then kept covered with the red earth above mentioned.
#Text=I never saw the effect, but the cure is said to be rapid.
#Text=In Dar-Fûr I have not observed the venereal disease more formidable than in Egypt.
#Text=I saw a few individuals who were mutilated in the organs of generation by its effects.
#Text=The old women, who are physicians in ordinary, use a decoction of certain roots, of which I never came at the knowlege, infused in bouza, which appear to operate successfully.
#Text=Gleets are frequent; and continued indulgence produces early debility and impotence.
#Text=The great advantages of the étuves, or warm baths, is evident in very many instances in Kahira.
#Text=But it is difficult to admit Savary’s assertion (vol. i. p. 108) in its full extent, viz. that they operate as a radical cure of the venereal disease.
#Text=They doubtless assuage many of its graver symptoms.
#Text=Bruises.

#Text=The bitumen found in the mummy pits is dissolved with butter by the Egyptians, and not only applied externally, but taken inwardly, in large doses, for bruises and wounds; it is said to have a surprising effect.
#Text=In no country are pulmonary diseases more rare than in Egypt, which could not happen if the baths had any tendency to cause them.
#Text=Natrôn.
#Text=Natrôn is much used as a veterinary medicine.
#Text=As often as the camels, horses, asses, sheep, &c. drink, a large piece of it is put into the trough of water.
#Text=The natives conceive that it renders them more eager of their food, and thus tends to fatten them.
#Text=Some camels refuse it, but in general they acquire a preference for that water which is most strongly impregnated.
#Text=When they refuse it, the natrôn is pulverized, formed into balls, with the flour of maize, and forced down their throats before they drink.
#Text=For the human race natrôn is used to remove the head-achs, intermittent and remittent fevers, &c. which prevail during the rainy season.
#Text=Two or three ounces of crude natrôn are dissolved in water, and taken fasting.
#Text=It operates as a drastic purge, and with some as an emetic.
#Text=With robust and plethoric habits, there seems to be no inconvenience from the use of it, but I experienced from it an unfavourable rather than beneficial effect.
#Text=Tamarinds.
#Text=The tamarind, Thummara Hindi[51] one of the most useful as well as valuable of the productions of the country, supplies the want of many others.
#Text=In defect of lemons and other acids, this fruit, mixed with water, constitutes an agreeable and refreshing drink.
#Text=When dried by beating in a mortar, it is formed into cakes, each of 2 or 300 drams in weight.
#Text=The decoction of it is a mild cathartic, and also operates as a diaphoretic; and the natives attribute to it superior virtue as an antidote against certain poisons.
#Text=As Kesrawân and Mount Libanus produce the best wines of Syria, it may not be improper to offer a few remarks on that topic.
#Text=The white wine made at Jerusalem has a sulphureous taste, and is very strong; the red somewhat resembles Tent, and is comparatively mild in its effects.

#Text=The wines of Syria are most of them prepared by boiling, immediately after they are expressed from the grape, till they be considerably reduced in quantity, when they are put into jars or large glass bottles, (damesjans,) and preserved for use.
#Text=There is reason to believe, that this mode of boiling their wines was in general practice among the antients.

#Text=It is still retained in some parts of Provence, where it is called vîn cuite, or cooked wine; but there the method is to lodge the wine in a large room, receiving all the smoke arising from several fires on the ground-floors; an operation more slow, but answering the same purpose.
#Text=The Spanish Vino Tinto, or Tent, is prepared in the same way.
#Text=The wines thus managed, are sometimes thickened so much as to lose their transparency, and acquire a sweetish taste.
#Text=Numerous are the kinds made in Syria; but the chief is the Vino d’Oro, or golden wine of Mount Libanus.
#Text=This is not boiled, but left to purify itself by keeping; the quantity produced is small.
#Text=It is, as the name implies, of a bright golden colour, and is highly prized even on the spot.
